persona.name: Jun-seo Park
persona.role: Robotics Engineer — BC PNP Tech applicant
persona.education: BEng Mechanical Engineering, KAIST (Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology), 2019

career.specialization: Industrial robot arm kinematics, computer vision integration, ROS2
notable_projects: Led design of HA006B collaborative robot arm
Computer vision QC system for Hyundai Motor assembly line

canadian_opportunity.title: Robotics Software Engineer
canadian_opportunity.why_recruited: Sanctuary AI building humanoid robots — needs kinematics expertise that's rare in Canada

persona.voice: Young, technically brilliant, slightly impatient with bureaucracy. Thinks in engineering terms — wants to optimize the process, understand the critical path, eliminate bottlenecks. First time navigating immigration — everything is new. Trusts systems more than people. Asks 'why' before 'how'. Motivated by the opportunity at Sanctuary AI, not just PR status.
priorities: Get to Vancouver as fast as possible — Sanctuary AI is waiting
CRS 430 isn't enough on its own — PNP nomination is the strategy
Keep the job offer alive — conditional offers can expire
Military service documentation — will Korea's mandatory service cause issues?
Don't overpay — $4,000-8,000 is a lot for a 29-year-old engineer
concerns: What if Sanctuary AI rescinds the offer during processing?
Is BC PNP Tech really as fast as they say?
My CRS is 430 — am I wasting money on Express Entry without PNP?
I did 18 months military service — does that affect anything?
Can I start working remotely for Sanctuary while I wait?
What happens if I want to change employers after I arrive?

narrator.voice: Third-person. The young skilled worker perspective — employer-driven, speed-focused, first-time immigrant navigating a system designed for families and entrepreneurs.
narrator.format: immigrant perspective + tech talent retention angle
